title: Untitled
research_problem: Investigating pedestrian behaviors at various urban crosswalks and pedestrian perceptions toward different pedestrian facilities (signalized/unsignalized intersection crosswalks, midblock crosswalks, physical barriers, shelters, colored paving) to help traffic engineers and planners design better pedestrian facilities
method_summary: Mixed-methods study combining: (1) observational study using video cameras to record pedestrian movements and calculate crossing compliance rates, and (2) user survey questionnaires distributed to 711 daily and occasional users of the study corridor to assess pedestrian perceptions and preferences
tasks: measure_spatial_crossing_compliance_rate; measure_temporal_crossing_compliance_rate; assess_pedestrian_perceptions_toward_facilities; identify_factors_influencing_crossing_choices; compare_survey_and_observational_data
datasets: Grand River Avenue corridor in East Lansing, MI (1-km divided boulevard from Abbott to Bogue Streets); Video data from 8 video cameras capturing pedestrian movements during peak hours (noon-peak and PM-peak periods); Survey responses from 711 pedestrians (254 daily users, 457 occasional users)
metrics: spatial_crossing_compliance_rate_SCCR; temporal_crossing_compliance_rate_TCCR; pedestrian_crossing_volume; user_survey_response_rates
claim_text: Unsignalized midblock crosswalks are the most preferred pedestrian facility (83% reported preference) with high crossing compliance (71.2%)
limitations: Study conducted at a single location (Grand River Avenue, East Lansing) near a university campus, limiting generalizability to other contexts | Survey sample primarily comprised university affiliates (students, staff, faculty), which may not represent general pedestrian population demographics | Data collection excluded rainy days and special events (football games, holidays), potentially biasing results toward typical conditions | Crosswalk influence area methodology assumes uniform pedestrian attraction within defined boundaries, which may oversimplify actual pedestrian behavior
notes: Study site featured multiple pedestrian facility types: signalized intersections (Abbott, Collingwood, MAC Avenue, Division), unsignalized T-intersections (Charles, Orchard), marked midblock crosswalks with/without shelters, non-striped midblock crosswalks with colored brick pavement, physical barriers (vegetation, concrete walls), and pedestrian warning signs. Statistical tests showed age and gender differences were not significant at 95% confidence level except for distance factor response among elderly pedestrians.

title: Untitled
research_problem: Modeling pedestrian road crossing behavior in traffic system micro-simulation in China, specifically addressing the high rate of signal non-compliance (red light running) which differs significantly from pedestrian behavior in developed countries
method_summary: A pedestrian model for traffic system micro-simulation that classifies pedestrians into two types: law-obeying (Type I) and opportunistic (Type II). The model considers external factors including policeman presence, behavior of other pedestrians, and vehicle flow. Parameters are calibrated using questionnaires (495 respondents in Xi'an) and videotape data extracted via image processing software to determine gap acceptance behavior. The safety criterion DT (time gap) is calculated based on pedestrian estimates of vehicle arrival time and crossing time.
tasks: Pedestrian red light running simulation; Gap acceptance decision modeling; Mixed traffic flow simulation
datasets: Questionnaire data from 495 respondents in Xi'an (200 students and 300 pedestrians); Videotape data from zebra crossing outside Southeast Gate of Xi'an Jiaotong University (989 samples of DT)
metrics: Proportion of red light jumpers; Proportion of violation followers; Time interval distribution of pedestrian arrivals
claim_text: The model successfully represents the high rate of pedestrians' red light running and mixed traffic characteristics specific to Chinese cities
limitations: Current model only models pedestrians' decision making according to instantaneous states of oncoming vehicles, not accounting for vehicles changing states (accelerating, decelerating) | Calculation method of DT needs improvement for turning vehicles to make the model more practical | Questionnaire respondents may not represent all pedestrians; answers may not reflect actual daily behavior, leading to initial simulation results deviating from field data
notes: The paper addresses a unique problem specific to developing countries - high pedestrian signal non-compliance rates. The model introduces 'violation followers' (pedestrians who follow others to violate traffic signal within 2 seconds). Parameter r2 was adjusted from 0.69 to 0.35 based on analyzing deviation causes. The study was conducted in Xi'an, China, with data collection from November 2003 to April 2004.

title: Untitled
research_problem: Examine pedestrians' self-reported violating crossing behavior intentions by applying and extending the Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB), adding descriptive norm, perceived risk, and conformity tendency as new factors to evaluate their respective impacts on pedestrian behavior intentions.
method_summary: Survey-based study using questionnaires with scenario-based items about pedestrian red-light violations at intersections. Data collected in Dalian, China with 260 valid responses. Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) was used to test four models: basic TPB model and three extended models adding descriptive norm, perceived risk, and conformity tendency.
method_family: Structural Equation Modeling (SEM), Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB) framework
tasks: Predicting pedestrian violating road-crossing behavior intentions
datasets: 260 complete and valid survey responses from pedestrians in Dalian, China
metrics: CMIN/df ratio; RMSEA; PCLOSE; CFI; Cronbach's alpha; Regression weights
claim_text: Instrumental attitude (perceived benefits like saving time or convenience) is a significant positive predictor of pedestrians' violating crossing behavior intention across all four models
limitations: Self-reported behavior may introduce bias, especially for negative behaviors; actual behavior should be observed for validation | Relatively small number of items in each construct to maintain internal reliability may affect results | Perceived behavioral control was not significant, possibly because the scenario was not sufficiently risky or the behavior was not challenging enough for the sample | Perceived risk was not a significant predictor, suggesting pedestrians may fail to realize or underestimate the danger of violating crossing behavior
notes: This study extends TPB by adding descriptive norm, perceived risk, and conformity tendency. The survey used a scenario where pedestrians cross against red light at an intersection. All respondents were physically able to cross, which may explain non-significance of perceived behavioral control. The sample was 57.3% female, with 49.2% aged 18-24 years, 58.5% with undergraduate education.

title: Untitled
research_problem: Understanding pedestrian street-crossing behavior at signalized intersections, specifically developing a statistical model for pedestrians' intended waiting times during the red-man phase of traffic light signals. The research addresses the gap that while pedestrian behavior has received attention, there has been little methodological development of pedestrian waiting time models at signalized intersections.
method_summary: The paper proposes a new statistical model for pedestrians' intended waiting times at signalized intersections. Starting from a simplified scenario for a particular pedestrian under specific traffic conditions using bounded Pareto distribution, the model incorporates vehicle time headway via a generalized linear model. Statistical unconditioning is applied to integrate the waiting-time distribution over the distribution of vehicle headway, resulting in a mixture distribution with four components. The EM algorithm is used for parameter estimation. The model produces a U-shaped distribution capturing the interaction between pedestrians and vehicles.
method_family: Statistical mixture modeling, bounded distribution models, EM algorithm
tasks: Modeling pedestrian intended waiting times at signalized intersections; Analyzing pedestrian crossing behavior by age and gender
datasets: Field data from signalized intersection in Kunming, China (283 valid observations collected on two consecutive weekend days)
metrics: Goodness-of-fit test (chi-square); Parameter estimates with standard errors; Proportion estimates (r1, r2, r4); Median and average waiting times
claim_text: The proposed statistical model produces a U-shaped distribution that characterizes pedestrian street-crossing behavior, showing that a large proportion of pedestrians cross immediately after arriving and a large proportion wait for the entire red-man phase
limitations: The model focuses on univariate analysis with only one variable (pedestrian waiting time) explicitly modeled | Most existing multivariate approaches assume normal or exponential distributions which are not suitable for the bounded waiting time problem, limiting ability to analyze multiple risk factors simultaneously
notes: The model was empirically validated with field data from Kunming, China. The 3-component discretized model (12) was selected over the 4-component model based on BIC criterion (1162.9 vs 1174.1). Goodness-of-fit test passed at 5% significance level (chi-square = 3.51, critical value = 7.81). Key finding: about 13.8% of pedestrians intended to cross immediately, 50.6% waited for entire phase, 35.6% waited then crossed.

metrics: Goodness-of-fit test (chi-square); Parameter estimates with standard errors; Proportion estimates (r1, r2, r4); Median and average waiting times

title: Untitled
research_problem: The research investigates factors affecting pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at signalized intersections in China, addressing the high rate of pedestrian-involved traffic crashes. Over 270,000 pedestrians die annually in traffic crashes worldwide, with pedestrian red-light running identified as a main cause of pedestrian crashes at intersection areas.
method_summary: A binomial logistic regression model was established using survey data collected from Hefei, China. The SP (stated preference) survey included 15 items collecting pedestrian socioeconomic characteristics, trip features, and attitude variables. Based on 631 valid samples, the Score test was used to identify significant variables, followed by iterative logistic regression to determine the final model with four significant predictors.
tasks: identify_significant_factors_affecting_pedestrian_red_light_running; predict_probability_of_red_light_running_behavior; evaluate_impacts_of_trip_characteristics_and_attitudes
datasets: Survey data from Hefei, China (631 valid samples); Collected May-June 2015; Six typical signalized intersections selected; Peak period调查 times: 7:30-8:30am, 11:30am-12:30pm, 6:00-7:00pm on weekdays and weekends
metrics: Score test significance (95% confidence level); Odds ratios; Model coefficients; P-values
claim_text: Four variables significantly affect pedestrian red-light running behavior: trip purpose, time period in a day, attitude towards running red-light when in hurry, and attitude towards whether road facility quality affects crossing behavior
limitations: Data collected from only one Chinese city (Hefei); results may not generalize to other cities or countries due to cultural and driving behavior differences | Study focused only on signalized intersections, not including other crossing facilities like midblock crossings with/without buttons | Individual crossing behaviors studied while ignoring impacts of pedestrian platoons/group behavior | Limited time and labor resources constrained data collection scope
notes: The study addresses a critical traffic safety issue in China where pedestrian-involved crashes account for a quarter of all traffic deaths. The binomial logistic model provides a probability prediction function using the four significant variables. The paper contributes by incorporating pedestrian attitude variables which were seldom considered in previous studies.

title: Untitled
research_problem: Identify personal and environmental factors affecting pedestrian red light running propensity at signalized intersections in urban Hong Kong, where pedestrian crashes constitute 25% of crashes at signalized intersections and 62% of road fatalities involve pedestrians.
method_summary: Video observation surveys conducted at 6 signalized crosswalks in urban Hong Kong during weekdays, capturing crossing behaviors of 6320 pedestrians across 652 signal cycles. A random parameter logit model was developed to measure associations between red light running propensity and explanatory factors, accounting for unobserved heterogeneity in pedestrian behavior.
tasks: analyze_pedestrian_red_light_running_behavior; identify_personal_factors_affecting_violation_propensity; identify_environmental_factors_affecting_violation_propensity; examine_interaction_effects_between_factors
datasets: 6320 pedestrians observed at 6 signalized crosswalks in Hong Kong; 652 signal cycles captured; 6 urban intersection sites with high pedestrian crash history (each >10 pedestrian crashes in 5 years)
metrics: red_light_running_violation_binary_outcome; odds_ratios; marginal_effects; elasticities; AIC_for_model_comparison
claim_text: Female pedestrians have significantly lower propensity of red light running compared to males (odds ratio = 0.667, 64.3% probability of lower propensity)
limitations: Study focuses on factors affecting red light running propensity but does not measure safety outcomes or associated crash risk | Effect of vehicular speed on pedestrian red light running behavior is not assessed despite its correlation with crash risk and severity | Data on pedestrian safety perception, attitude, and socioeconomic characteristics (education, income) are not available in the observation survey | Random parameter approach accounts for unobserved heterogeneity but cannot fully capture individual-specific factors like personality traits and risk attitudes
notes: Study addresses gap in literature by examining both individual-level (pedestrian demographics) and cycle-level (traffic condition, signal time) factors together; Novel contribution includes examining influence of social norms through presence and behavior of other pedestrians; Hong Kong pedestrian signals have three phases: steady green, flashing green, and steady red - no countdown display exists; Model 2 with interaction effects (gender x traffic volume, gender x others violated, elderly x companion, elderly x others violated) provides superior fit (AIC: 2750.1 vs 2776.6); Random parameter for gender effect is statistically significant, justifying use of random parameter logit to account for unobserved heterogeneity

title: Untitled
research_problem: Understanding pedestrian crossing behavior at signalized intersections under mixed traffic conditions in India, including crossing speed, signal compliance, and pedestrian-vehicular interaction
method_summary: Video-based field observation of 775 pedestrians at 3 signalized intersections in Mumbai during peak hours. Statistical analysis using ANOVA, Pearson correlation coefficient, and Student t tests to identify significant factors. Logistic regression models developed to predict pedestrian noncompliance and pedestrian-vehicular interaction.
tasks: pedestrian_crossing_speed_analysis; pedestrian_compliance_behavior_analysis; pedestrian-vehicular_interaction_analysis
datasets: 775 pedestrian samples from 3 signalized intersections in Mumbai (Swami Vivekanand Junction, Mahim Junction, Holkar Junction)
metrics: crossing_speed_(m/s); compliance_rate_(%); CSDF_(Crossing_Speed_Deviation_Factor); odds_ratio_(OR)
claim_text: Pedestrian crossing speed at signalized intersections varies significantly from the IRC standard of 1.2 m/s, with an observed average of 1.34 m/s
limitations: Study limited to only 3 signalized intersections in Mumbai, India - may not generalize to other cities or regions | Data collected only during morning and evening peak hours - does not capture off-peak behavior | Sample size of 775 pedestrians may not be sufficiently large for robust modeling of all behavioral factors
notes: Study introduces Crossing Speed Deviation Factor (CSDF) as a new metric; Logistic regression models developed for both noncompliance and interaction prediction; Design crossing speeds determined: 0.95 m/s for old pedestrians, 1.12 m/s for adult pedestrians; Only 44% of pedestrians complied with traffic signals - high noncompliance in Mumbai; Mixed traffic conditions characterized by diverse vehicle types (car, auto/van, taxi, two-wheeler, HCV)

title: Untitled
research_problem: Developing a probabilistic model to predict pedestrian crossing decisions at signalized intersections for connected vehicle applications, specifically to reduce collisions between pedestrians and turning vehicles at intersections
method_summary: A Dynamic Bayesian Network (DBN) model that probabilistically integrates relationships among traffic signal context, pedestrian decisions (cross/wait), motion types (standing/walking/running), and physical movement. Uses particle filter for Bayesian filtering to estimate pedestrian states including position, crossing decision, and motion type from traffic signal phases and pedestrian position observations.
method_family: Probabilistic Graphical Models (Dynamic Bayesian Networks) with Particle Filter Inference
tasks: Pedestrian crossing decision recognition (cross vs wait); Motion type classification (standing, walking, running); Pedestrian position estimation
datasets: 289 pedestrian sequences collected from a vehicle at a signalized intersection in Japan. Contains pedestrian trajectories, motion types, and traffic signal phases. 251 cross-pedestrians and 38 wait-pedestrians. Used 4-fold cross-validation for training/testing.
metrics: Confusion matrices for decision recognition; Confusion matrices for motion recognition; Precision values for decision and motion; Positioning error (mean and standard deviation); Recognition accuracy at different time-from-decision (TFD) moments
claim_text: The proposed DBN+PF system recognizes pedestrian crossing decision with >85% accuracy after 2 seconds of observation and wait decision with 85% accuracy after 3-4 seconds across different noise levels
limitations: The model assumes straight-line pedestrian trajectories and cannot handle curved trajectories well, causing positioning errors in turn-cross scenarios | Running motion is difficult to distinguish from walking motion due to overlapping speed distributions, with only 35-56% recognition rate for running | Larger observation noises cause delayed reaction in recognizing running motion and earlier false detection of standing, degrading motion recognition performance | The assumption that pedestrians make decisions at the onset of PFG may not always hold; pedestrians may change decisions after onset based on traffic situations | The model only considers distance to crosswalk as explanatory variable; additional contextual factors like crosswalk length, vehicle presence, and other environmental factors are needed for more accurate modeling | Wait decision recognition takes longer (3-4s) than cross decision (2s) because pedestrians do not always decelerate immediately after deciding to wait
notes: Research focuses on left-hand traffic (Japan) and near-side pedestrians approaching left-turning vehicles. Uses Japanese pedestrian signal phases: PG (pedestrian green), PFG (pedestrian flashing green), and PR (pedestrian red). The model considers that during PG pedestrians always have cross intention, and decisions are made at PFG onset. Computational efficiency: 5.1s to process 1.0s data (13 frames) using 2000 particles.

title: Untitled
research_problem: Automating the generation of literature review segments from PDF files of research papers, addressing the challenge of time-consuming and error-prone manual literature reviews due to the exponential growth in scientific publications
method_summary: Three approaches are implemented and compared: (1) frequency-based extractive summarization using spaCy with word frequency calculation and sentence weight ranking, (2) transformer-based abstractive summarization using Simple T5 fine-tuned on SciTLDR, (3) retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) using GPT-3.5-TURBO-0125 with SciTLDR as knowledge base
method_family: Extractive summarization; Abstractive summarization; Retrieval-augmented generation
tasks: Automated literature review segment generation from PDF inputs
datasets: SciTLDR dataset (Hugging Face) - 5,400 TLDRs from over 3,200 scientific papers with source (abstract, introduction, conclusion) and target (summaries) fields
metrics: ROUGE-1 (precision, recall, F1); ROUGE-2 (precision, recall, F1); ROUGE-L (longest common subsequence); ROUGE-Lsum (summary-level evaluation)
claim_text: The LLM-based model (GPT-3.5-turbo) achieved the highest ROUGE-1 score of 0.364, outperforming T5 (0.268) and spaCy (0.257) in generating literature reviews
limitations: The models are not yet fully capable of automating literature reviews and require human reviewers (from related work by Yuan et al.) | The paper lacks practical validation across diverse domains | The system only generates literature review segments (summary of individual papers), not comprehensive literature review sections with synthesis across papers | ROUGE scores may not fully capture semantic quality and coherence of generated literature reviews
notes: The SciTLDR dataset contains both author-written and expert-derived TLDRs of scientific documents; All three systems take PDF files or DOIs as input and produce summarized literature review segments; The final GUI tool was developed using GPT-3.5-TURBO-0125 as the backend; The ROUGE evaluation was performed on test data from the SciTLDR dataset; spaCy uses top 10% of sentences based on calculated sentence weights as final output
